317.title: Something's Killing Me
317.description: Changes started subtly for twins Addi and Cassi, but after medical testing it was discovered they had a strange, genetic disease that causes progressive neurological deterioration.

437.title: Something's Killing Me
437.description: Mark Staudte abruptly became ill, experiencing seizures and his skin turning yellow. Within 48 hours, Mark died, and five months later, his son developed similar symptoms.

481.title: Something's Killing Me
481.description: Seven Chicago residents died under mysterious medical circumstances. Diagnoses included cardiac arrest, stroke and aneurism. However, a connection is made to all seven victims.

523.title: Something's Killing Me
523.description: Jeanna Giese complains to her parents that she's fatigued and has numbness in her arm. Doctors speculate that it might be Lyme disease or West Nile, but what Jeanna has is far more sinister.

564.title: Something's Killing Me
564.description: Florida college student Lindsey Meyer and Michigan E.M.T Kevin Nesbit both come down with a painful sore throat. What seem like symptoms of a common cold, soon spiral into a life or death situation.

7063.title: Something's Killing Me
7063.description: Paul and Alison Frase were told their child's disorder had no treatment and no cure. Scientists tell them their one hope depends on finding a dog and the right genetic code - the search begins.

7180.title: Something's Killing Me
7180.description: Dr. Bill Lidner collapses after returning home from a family trip overseas; his daughters find him bleeding; they don't know it, but it's the first sign that something's very wrong.

7221.title: Something's Killing Me
7221.description: Several doctors and CDC authorities spend months trying to figure out what California honeymooners, a surfer in Hawaii and a toddler in Tennessee have in common.

7262.title: Something's Killing Me
7262.description: What really happens when we die? For thousands of years, the dreams and visions of the dying have captivated cultures. But what are they - what causes them? And are they real?
